Wonderful catch.

Fixed in furl v0.4.95.

```python
>>> import furl
>>> furl.__version__
'0.4.95'
>>> from furl import furl
>>> f = furl('https://MyHostWithCaps.example.org')
>>> f.url
'https://myhostwithcaps.example.org'
>>> f.host = 'MyHostWithCaps.example.org'
>>> f.url
'https://myhostwithcaps.example.org'
```

Upgrade with `pip install furl -U`.

Thank you for the thorough, detailed Issue and bringing this bug to my
attention, Vincent. Don't hesitate to let me know if there's anything else I can

It looks like the latest version of numpy (1.11) adds a new magic method named `__numpy_ufunc__`. If an  instance has this method, it will be called and its return value will be returned to the user. This should make it possible for the `Measurement` class to build on top of the `Quantity` classes `numpy` support. Currently, `__array_prepare__` has to return an `ndarray` (or a subclass), so you can't just use the `Quantity` classes implementation. With the new `__numpy_ufunc__` method, that should be possible.
Agreed!

```

Cool thank you. Merging into develop.

> `flake8 .\wsgidav\ --count --max-line-length=99 --statistics`

Oh, I had no idea about those arguments, how handy! Thanks.

> E713 test for membership should be 'not in' I am not yet convinced that 'a not in b' should be enforced over 'not a in b'

They are exactly equivalent at the byte code level (they both use the `not in` operation), but `a not in b` is considered to be more readable than `not a in b`. I'm not overly fussed.

> E501 line too long (100 > 99 characters)
> even if I would expect coders to try to stick with 80 chars, we should only enforce '<= 99'

I think that's fine, let's add a `[flake8]` section to the `setup.cfg`

> E402 module level import not at top of file
> Havn't looked at the concrete lines, but there may be reasons to do this (e.g. fallbacks for different Python versions or optional libraries) and to prevent recursive import errors

One option to automatically fix these is `isort`, [check the results here](https://github.com/mar10/wsgidav/compare/master...tomviner:run-isort). That fixed all the E402 errors, (except the `conf.py` file we want to ignore) but we should check we're happy with the results on a case by case basis. I can make that branch into a PR for review?

> F841 local variable _res is assigned to but never used
> PyDev silences warnings if the variable starts with '_', which I like because it allows to have variables for debugging

Anyone know how to exclude variables by name in `[flake8]` config? Or perhaps just add `# NOQA` to those lines?
The reason I'm against turning the rule off, is it often points out a programming mistake where you actually meant to use that variable but forgot.

> Conf.py and compat.py should have an # flake8: noqa or s.th. similar at the top

Agreed.

I don't understand your first response, and wasn't aware of deriving a nonce from a message. I know that changing the nonce would cause decryption to fail. An attacker truncating a message and causing a crash, while it does not expose the message, is still a nuance. If this was on a web-exposed system it would allow anyone to crash your server...

The reason that I raised this concern is because, while a C developer is aware of these issues, Python developers don't tend to think about range checks as the language does it most of the time. Access past the end of an array in C you get a segfault or corrupt the stack, the classic buffer overflow. If you access past the end of a python list you get a catchable exception.

As a "simple"-ish example of what can be done, consider:

```
f(s) = 60 b06 + 45 b16 + 32 b26 + 21 b36 + 12 b46 + 5 b56
     = 30 s^2 - 90 s + 60
     = 30 (s - 2) (s - 1)
```

i.e.

```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> import bezier
>>>
>>> nodes = np.asfortranarray([
...     [60.0],
...     [45.0],
...     [32.0],
...     [21.0],
...     [12.0],
...     [ 5.0],
...     [ 0.0],
... ])
>>> polynomial = bezier.Curve(nodes, degree=1, _copy=False)
>>> s_vals = np.arange(7.0)
>>> s_vals
array([ 0.,  1.,  2.,  3.,  4.,  5.,  6.])
>>> def f(s):
...     return 30 * (s - 2) * (s - 1)
... 
>>> f(s_vals)
array([  60.,   -0.,    0.,   60.,  180.,  360.,  600.])
>>> polynomial.evaluate_multi(s_vals).flatten()
array([  60.,    0.,    0.,   60.,  180.,  360.,  600.])
```

Hi Faulik.
The short answer is you should load the grammars like this:  g=plyplus.Grammar(c, auto_filter_tokens=False).

The long answer is that there is a basic mechanism in plyplus to filter out "noise" tokens (for example in "a+(b)" the brackets are useless after parsing). The mechanism works this way: In rules that are dedicated to a single token, keep the token (for example: "plus: PLUS"), but otherwise remove all tokens. So in our example, it would parse as "var plus LPAR var RPAR" and plyplus will get rid of the parenthesis while keeping the plus sign).
So one thing you could do is rewrite your grammar so that everything that is important to you (SECTION, OPTION, VALUE) is protected within it's own rule. The other thing you can do is add the auto_filter_tokens option, which disables that mechanism and keeps ALL tokens. It means you will have to clean-up the tree for yourself (or search it more carefully), but all of your important values will be there.

I hope this helps,
Erez

Hi Rob,
Entropy and enthalpy are always calculated with respect to a reference point. To compare values from different sources, one must always use only changes in values, not absolute values. REFPROP has much more accurate values of enthalpy than thermo; thermo has only a cubic EOS behind it, whereas theirs is a custom EOS just for ammonia. I'd hope it wouldn't be too much of a difference though.

The issue with the solver is indeed a phase change you are observing; the entropy is discontinuous at the phase change, as shown in this crude plot. 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16520296/32475129-a3eee7ba-c346-11e7-9a00-e6110cede9f2.png)
You seem comfortable with the library; I'd encourage you to use the HeatCapacityLiquid and HeatCapacityGas (use the T_dependent_property_integral and T_dependent_property_integral_over_T methods) along with the eos departure enthalpy/entropies, and build your own entropy/enthalpy reference frame you can fully understand and be confident in from the pieces. It's a great learning experience! Feel free to ask more questions.
Sincerely,
Caleb

The issue looks like it is caused by the Layer.__init__() method throwing away the tree structure of the xml_obj by using xml_obj.findall('.//field'), rather than recursing the children via xml_obj.iterchildren('field')

One possible solution is to refactor the LayerField object to have a self.children member of a LayerFieldContainer, and then update the properties and repr accordingly. This might be simpler if the Layer.__init__ took a list of Fields (possibly empty) rather than an xml_obj directly.

There are many possible solutions to the internal representation though, so I leave it up to your judgement. Kindly...

Currently, ml-ensemble only handles numerical class labels. In this case, the class label corresponds to the column index, so there shouldn't be any need to access a ``classes_`` attribute.  For instance, an ensemble of two estimators with 2 class labels would have the following column structure:

| col=0   | col=1     | col=2   | col=3    |
| ------ | ------- | ------ | ------- |
| est=0   | est=0     | est=1   | est=1    |
| label=0 | label=1 | label=0 | label=1 |

If you really want to access the ``classes_`` attribute of an estimator, you can with

```python
sl.layer_1.learners[0]._learner_[0].estimator.classes_
```
Adjust for the layer and estimator you want to look at. Note that the backend structure may change so this way of access the ``classes_`` attribute can break without warning.

It seems that aiohttp keeps reverting its stance on whether `ClientSession.close` is a coroutine. I've been using `aiohttp` in this library since it was in v0.20 or so.

During that time, from v0.20 to around v0.22 it was not a coroutine so this solution did indeed work. When v1.x series came out, they decided to make it a coroutine, except this time it was a dud coroutine since all it did was return a completed `asyncio.Future`.

Now we're in v2.x and it seems to me that they've reverted this decision and now it is no longer a coroutine, even giving a deprecation warning about it:

```py
    def close(self):
        """Close underlying connector.

        Release all acquired resources.
        """
        # actual code goes here -- omitted for brevity
        return deprecated_noop('ClientSession.close() is not coroutine')
```

Anywho, this is the history why historically the solution you propose has never been added.

I'm not sure if I should decide to add it at all. On one hand it would suppress the ClientSession is not closed message, however that message seems to have a dual purpose of informing me when people don't actually run their bots -- since during normal execution the client will have cleaned itself up naturally.

IMO the reporter had a point. With sshfs it is hard to deal with errors. When error happens, I usually need to umount and remount, and possibly kill the sshfs process beforehand. And if there's an acme_tiny process hanging, then I have to hunt it down before I can umount. If there's hundreds of server, then it is going to be an administrative nightmare. What we need is a way to automate the case where the machine running acme is different from the machine hosting the challenge files, in the cleanest possible way.

Instead of having a hook after saving challenge file, I'm proposing creating --acme-savecommand that can be used in place of --acme-dir. Example:

```
python acme_tiny.py \
    --account-key ./account.key \
    --csr ./domain.csr \
    --acme-dir /var/www/challenges/ \
    > ./signed.crt
```

can be rewritten as:

```
python acme_tiny.py \
    --account-key ./account.key \
    --csr ./domain.csr \
    --acme-savecommand "echo %(content) > /var/www/challenges/%(filename)" \
    > ./signed.crt
```

for remote operation, one would use something like:

```
python acme_tiny.py \
    --account-key ./account.key \
    --csr ./domain.csr \
    --acme-savecommand "echo %(content) | ssh user@remoteserver 'cat > /var/www/challenges/%(filename)'" \
    > ./signed.crt
```
LE does not allow this. See https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/support-for-ports-other-than-80-and-443/3419

To answer your concerns:

1. Scrypt can use [MCF](https://pythonhosted.org/passlib/modular_crypt_format.html) which is the standard way of storing hashes (it is stored with an identifier for the algorithm used, so that it is backward and forward compatible).  Unfortunately, [glances currently stores `salt$password`](https://github.com/nicolargo/glances/blob/fd6c7a8934065108cc8f7b0ab3a2fa4ccf49e2f9/glances/core/glances_password.py#L85) which is non standard to my knowledge, so we would have to for example to use a new file name to discriminate between pre and post MCF. **What software third party are you mentioning, that you want to remain compatible with?**
2. Did you read some doc or the Wikipedia page about Scrypt? It is designed **precisely** so you can configure the CPU time and memory needed to calculate a hash. If it is too slow, just decrease  `n`, `r` or `P`.

It seems interesting but I don't know if I get the whole point of this idea. Does `values` is supposed to:
0. get the list of deserialised values (results of calling `value()` for each one of them)?
0. get list of raw strings from query string params (via request `req.get_param_as_list()`) 

If this is (1.) then I think that this is almost the same solution as the proposed with `container` but named differently.

If it is (2.) then I think that right now there is very limited gain from such splitting of implementation. I worry that this would make easier for users to take bad design decisions about how their params work. Generally param instances are used to describe parsing/deserialization of single query string parameter and such behaviour should not change with custom `many` setting.

Anyway, I do not say definitive no to any of these options. I think we should keep such idea in mind and revisit it later in future if someone finds really practical use case for that.

The reason only 6 priorities are defined is because I wanted to keep the settings similar to jQuery Mobile's table widget. This widget combines both the [table column toggle](http://demos.jquerymobile.com/1.4.5/table-column-toggle/) and [table reflow](http://demos.jquerymobile.com/1.4.5/table-reflow/) functions.

Personally, I think 15 priorities is too many. The reason there are only a few is because it's more work defining a layout for each priority (screen width). And there really aren't that many [differences in screen widths](https://design.google.com/devices/) that need to be addressed. So if the table has 15 priorities, what about the rest of the page?

That being said, you are more than welcome to do whatever you want. I have no issues with adding a new option that lets you set the number of priorities. That way you won't need to go in and edit the widget after each update.

This is an interesting idea.  I quite like the flat all the modules are in one directory approach that we currently have but we are now at ~75 so maybe we need to start thinking about splitting them.  I also like the idea of categorizing the modules and this would help there.

I think it's easier and better to deprecate few configs and a placeholder in `sysdata` (they all are there to display one thing and is not part of default format anyway) in favor of this module because this module just expose everything (eg all options) and is done nicely imho.

Also, it is technically possible to merge `nvidia_smi (all options) `, `nvidia_temp (one option)` in `sysdata` too... as well as new `hddtemp` and any other new monitoring modules in the future... and other existing modules like `netdata`, `net_rate`. To be honest, we probably could toss `uname` module in `sysdata` too.

All of this is going to be with one `cache_timeout` too. Customizing is limited when users want different `cache_timeout` for different placeholders... so I think it would be good to keep them separate, clean, and well organized/documented.... versus making new config with different placeholders this time with huge documentation for everything in one module.

>I don't understand exactly what you're trying to do.
1) If we merge this module, I want to deprecate the sysdata's `temp_unit` (config), `zone` (config), and `{cpu_temp}` (placeholder) to keep things clean.
2) My stance on `{load1}`, `{load5}`, `{load15}` placeholders versus the new `loadavg` module with customized threshold (which is just a loadavg value divided by cpu_core) is up in the air.... Seeing how `i3status` have its own `load` module, it may be nice to have our own `load` module too.
3) I hadn't looked at `net_data` and `net_rate` yet. I know they both read off a same `/proc` file so it may be possible to merge both... and to display any missing placeholders or options somehow. Maybe not. I'm not touching them as I want things to be merged, issues to be closed, etc first.

Also, I'm not trying to break things. I'm attempting to fix things up nicely... to expose more things and by exposing more things, sometimes it is better for new things to be in their own modules (with clean documentations and examples) and to just deprecate the old things.

BBDuk calculates average quality score by converting to probability scale, taking an average, and then converting back to Phred scale.  So for example, a 2bp read with quality scores 10 and 20 would yield an average quality of (0.9+0.99)/2=0.945 -> Q12.6 rather than Q15 with a linear average.

The reason for the 0.5 offset is because BBDuk is rounding the average quality to an int before comparing it to the threshold.  I could change it to a float; there's no particular reason for the rounding.

Great, I had been wondering about JIT for these expressions for a bit now. One thing to consider is `numba` or `numexpr` as well.

So the reason is that the ``removed_size`` of a outer product is always 1 and thus less than an inner product (or equal if the dimension is 1), and the ``greedy`` code prioritizes this over any flop cost.

I think this is sensible, there might be lots of cheap outer products which are overall bad, and knowing which *are* useful would require looking forward to subsequent potential contractions, like in the ``optimal`` code. [As an aside, this might be a nice mid-point between ``greedy`` and ``optimal``, you check every sequence of *two* contractions, the first of which can be an outer, then choose the best].

I think this also means that (apart from the case of size 1 indices), there is no change in the paths ``greedy`` is finding - outer products will still be optimized when they are all that remains.

Hi @gismcieri, took the liberty of refactoring your code. Please take a look below:

I used requests because it is pretty simple and standard to use it for doing HTTP requests than using urllib.
Also, if you will notice, I used `Json2xml.fromstring` instead of `Json2xml.fromurl` as I already got the response in String. `fromurl` is used when you are using Json2xml to directly `GET` the URL and convert to XML.

I have changed the filename to Unix style, please since I am running code on Mac, please change it as per your preference accordingly. Also, one small tip. Always close the file while opening and `with open('/tmp/temp.json', 'w') as f:` is kind of the standard practice.

```python
import json
import urllib.parse as urlparse
import requests

from src.json2xml import Json2xml

inPts = {'geometryType': 'esriGeometryPoint',
         'spatialReference': {'wkid': 54003},
         'features': [{'geometry': {'x': -13308192.1956127, 'y': 4221903.58555983}}]}

dist = {'distance': 8.5, 'units': 'esriMiles'}

data = {'Input_Observation_Point': inPts,
        'Viewshed_Distance': dist,
        'f': 'pjson'}

params = urlparse.urlencode(data)
params = params.encode('ascii')  # ('utf-8')
URL = 'http://sampleserver1.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/Elevation/ESRI_Elevation_World/GPServer/Viewshed/execute'

response = requests.get(URL, params)

result = response.json()

with open('/tmp/temp.json', 'w') as f:
    f.write(json.dumps(result))
f.close()

data = Json2xml.fromstring(json.dumps(result)).data
data_object = Json2xml(data)
xml_output = data_object.json2xml()

with open('/tmp/result.xml', 'w') as f:
    f.write(str(xml_output))
f.close()

```

Hope this helps!

You raise several good points and this is an issue I haven't really convinced myself either way on yet as there are merits to both approaches as it is a bit of a philosophical point. It isn't too late to change the approach, however the key I think will to pick one style over the other, and make changes where necessary to ensure that style is enforced for each function which will take some time to do right. Yes it would be more Spice like to return null values however that is because it is C/Fortran style which does not translate cleanly to idiomatic python, at least from what I have seen.

The returning Nones vs tuples of results is something I initially liked because it is similar in a way to an `Option` (IMHO) in Scala. Effectively a Spice function either returns a *something* or a *nothing*. If instead we always returning some value it makes the user work a bit harder to double check things, but perhaps that is necessary in either case.

Here's the updated code:
`#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
"""
Retrieve information from VOC
"""

import logging
from datetime import timedelta
import requests
try:
    from urlparse import urljoin
except ImportError:
    from urllib.parse import urljoin

_LOGGER = logging.getLogger(__name__)

SERVICE_URL = 'https://vocapi.wirelesscar.net/customerapi/rest/v3.0/'
HEADERS = {"X-Device-Id": "Device",
           "X-OS-Type": "Android",
           "X-Originator-Type": "App",
		   "Content-Type": "application/json",
		   "X-OS-Version": "19"}

TIMEOUT = timedelta(seconds=5)


class Connection():
    """Connection to the VOC server."""

    def __init__(self, username, password):
        """Initialize."""
        self._session = requests.Session()
        self._session.headers.update(HEADERS)
        self._session.auth = (username,
                              password)
        self._state = None

    def getquery(self, ref, rel=SERVICE_URL):
        """Perform a query to the online service."""
        url = urljoin(rel, ref)
        _LOGGER.debug("Request for %s", url)
        res = self._session.get(url, timeout=TIMEOUT.seconds)
        res.raise_for_status()
        _LOGGER.debug("Received %s", res.json())
        return res.json()

    def postquery(self, ref, rel=SERVICE_URL):
        """Perform a query to the online service."""
        url = urljoin(rel, ref)
        _LOGGER.debug("Post for %s", url)
        res = self._session.post(url, "{}")
        res.raise_for_status()
        _LOGGER.debug("Received %s", res.json())
        return res.json()

    def update(self, reset=False):
        """Update status."""
        try:
            _LOGGER.info("Updating1")
            if not self._state or reset:
                _LOGGER.info("Querying vehicles")
                user = self.getquery("customeraccounts")
                self._state = {}
                for vehicle in user["accountVehicleRelations"]:
                    rel = self.getquery(vehicle)
                    vehicle = rel["vehicle"] + '/'
                    state = self.getquery("attributes", vehicle)
                    self._state.update({vehicle: state})
            _LOGGER.debug("Updating2")
            for vehicle in self._state:
                status = self.getquery("status", vehicle)
                position = self.getquery("position", vehicle)
                heater = self.postquery("preclimatization/start", vehicle)
                vehicle = self._state[vehicle]
                vehicle.update(status)
                vehicle.update(position)
                vehicle.update(heater)
            _LOGGER.debug("State: %s", self._state)
            return True, self._state.values()
        except requests.exceptions.RequestException as error:
            _LOGGER.error("Could not query server: %s", error)
            return False, self._state.values()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    from os import path
    from sys import argv
    from pprint import pprint
#    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.CRITICAL)
#    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.ERROR)
#    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.WARNING)
#    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
#    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.NOTSET)


    def credentials():
        """Return user, pass."""
        if len(argv) == 3:
            return argv[1:]
        try:
            from yaml import safe_load as load_yaml
            with open(path.join(path.dirname(argv[0]),
                                ".credentials.yaml")) as config:
                config = load_yaml(config)
                return config["username"], config["password"]
        except ImportError:
            _LOGGER.error("Incorrect parameters. \nUsage: volvooncall.py <username> <password>")
            exit(-1)

    res, value = Connection(*credentials()).update()
    if res:
        pprint(list(value))
`

Hello @boonyao1122, sorry for keeping you waiting. Files are written to a new directory, which is by default a pytest session's tempdir:
https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/tmpdir.html

Each pytest run (i.e. "session") logs are written anew to a different dir.
```
$ pytest > /dev/null ; ll logs
lrwxrwxrwx 1 aurzenligl aurzenligl 39 Jun 18 02:39 logs -> /tmp/pytest-of-aurzenligl/pytest-3/logs
$ pytest > /dev/null ; ll logs
lrwxrwxrwx 1 aurzenligl aurzenligl 39 Jun 18 02:39 logs -> /tmp/pytest-of-aurzenligl/pytest-4/logs
```

If user chooses own dir, which is the same during each run, files will get overwritten. It doesn't seem to make sense to append to them, as output would get mixed in a way making it difficult to figure out which run given log line comes from.

2.0.8 is fine. Stick with that if you need this functionality.

There seems to be no point registering the signal handler in your
middleware, though. Once a hander is registered, it stays registered for
the whole app. You're just re-registering it once per request.

I still don't think you need to save a revision on delete, though. In terms
of your points:

   1. If you need to archive old revision data, consider using `./manage.py
   deleterevisions --keep=1 --days=30`. That will always keep at least one
   revision per object, purging ones older than 30 days. That way, you have no
   reason to save a revision on delete, since each model will keep at least
   one entry.
   2. Consider using a dedicated table for tracking who deleted something.
   Or, use the admin.LogEntry model for this, with a signal handler.
   3. Won't all related instances already be grouped in a revision when any
   of them are modified?

Thing is, reversion used to have the behaviour you've added, but it was
removed in 1.8 for a number of good reasons:
https://github.com/etianen/django-reversion/issues/164

It's your project, though. Just be aware of some possible side-effects.

You have to distinguish between what's provided by the swagger specifications and what's Swagger UI is being able to display.

Right now, Swagger UI only display a single textarea field for JSON input (see: #11).

You're right, there is no JSON input payload validation yet, but I will provide this feature (very) soon.

In the meantime, I advise you to write this:
```python
package = api.model('Package', {
    'type': fields.String(description="The type of package to create.", required=True)
})

class MyResource(Resource):
    @api.doc(expect=package)
    def get(self):
         '''Some doc'''
         data = request.json
         ''' do some validation'''
```
It will ensure documentation is well set.

In my experience, I prefer to keep the parser for querystring parsing.
RequestParser is not very effective on parsing `application/json` payloads.
It is meant to parse the root level of JSON payloads, not to parse an entire model (sadly).

For your alternative to work you need to write it like that:
```python
packages_post = api.parser()
packages_post.add_argument(
     'type',
     type=str,
     description='The type of package to create',
     required=True)

class MyResource(Resource):
    @api.doc(parser=packages_parser)
    def post(self):
         '''Some doc'''
         args = packages_post.parse_args()
         typ = args['type']
```

The issue is that Microsoft SQL supports a maximum precision of 35 digits, but the python decimal.Decimal type can exceed that depending on the float you feed it. I've fixed this for me by using the float type instead. (See above issue in pyodbc)

However, I feel like if this Microsoft SQL-specific module is to support the decimal.Decimal type, then we should handle this limitation somehow. Maybe with an error, maybe with rounding to 35 digits, I dunno. 

I am inclined to round to 35 digits, but I'm an outsider and probably don't know the best approach for this issue. I feel a bit icky about rounding when it comes to database integrity, but then maybe we shouldn't support the decimal type?

The use case I have for it is if a refresh token for a user was stolen. That could happen with a bug in the frontend application, or perhaps a situation like an employee that was fired had an existing login to the system. 

We can disable the user or change their password, but whoever has that refresh token can still freely access all of the endpoints that the user could access (except for the ones using fresh_jwt_required). At that point, the only way to stop them (well, not the only way, see bellow) would be to change the secret_key, which would invalidate their token. It would also have the side effect of invalidating every other token created by the system, in effect logging everyone out.

If the blacklist is enabled, we can go in and blacklist that single token. It will prevent them from being able to access the system any more, while not logging every other user out of the system.

This same effect could be done by checking the user rights on the database, and in many cases that may make more sense to do. That does have the side effect of checking a database for every endpoint though, which depending on the application could be expensive. By setting the access tokens to a very low expires time, and only checking the refresh token, we can get avoid the db/redis/whatever lookup costs the majority of the time.

That said, this is totally an optional feature, which is disabled by default :)

有很多软件是用DirectUI开发的，不是用微软提供的GUI开发框架开发的。
大多数的DirectUI都不支持UIAutomation，只有很少量的DirectUI支持UIAutomation，比如QQ，Firefox，Qt 
如果要让DirectUI程序支持UIAutomation，DirectUI程序的作者必须实现UI Automation Provider，
DirectUI程序实现了UI Automation Provider，才能支持UIAutomation。

https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ee671597(v=vs.85).aspx

If your application includes any custom controls, you need to implement UI Automation providers for those controls to make them accessible to accessibility client applications. You also need to implement providers for any third party controls that do not include a provider. You implement a provider by implementing UI Automation provider interfaces and control pattern interfaces.

Thanks for the feedback. Why would two transforms that are not inverses of each other cause bouncing behaviour?

I think that this is an useful feature. For example, say that I define a span selector and I want to fix its with without changing the class definition. With ``transform`` it is as simple as:

```python

import traitlets

class SpanSelector(traitlets.HasTraits):
    x1 = traitlets.Float()
    x2 = traitlets.Float()

ss = SpanSelector()
fix_width = traitlets.link((ss, "x1"), (ss, "x2"), (lambda x: x + 10, lambda x: x - 10))

```

Of course things may not work as expected if the transforms are not inverses, but I think that it is fair to assume that the users know what they're doing.

After digging around and debugging for a while, the issue might be within `billiard.heap` who's `BufferWrapper` allocates the same block twice. This is caused by the wrappers destructor being called (= memory freed) after no reference is alive anymore. 

[`billiard.sharedctypes.rebuild_ctype`](https://github.com/celery/billiard/blob/master/billiard/sharedctypes.py#L158) (which as far as I can tell constructs the object from the buffer) differentiates between Python 2 and 3. In Python 2 a reference to the wrapper is stored in the object, in Python 3 that reference is lost causing the wrapper to be garbage collected. I guess this is just an indentation error created while upgrading the library to Python 3.

**Easier example to reproduce**

The following code should output `0` (it does so for `multiprocessing`) but outputs `1` instead, indicating that the variable `done` was changed as a side effect.

```python
import ctypes
from billiard import Pool, Value


def foo():
    value.value = 1


def bar():
    print(done.value)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    value = Value(ctypes.c_int, 0)
    done = Value(ctypes.c_int, 0)

    with Pool() as pool:
        pool.apply(foo)
        pool.apply(bar)
```

@terminalmage I believe the performance implications and differences between the various git object DB backends are best explained [in the respective short section in the docs](http://gitpython.readthedocs.org/en/stable/tutorial.html#object-databases).

The differences will only affect object access, such reading a blob from a tree. If the `GitCmdObjectDB` is used, there will be two child processes (`git-cat-file`) ready to stream data. If the file is big, the process will temporarily allocate a buffer as big as the file itself. `GitDB` doesn't do that as it is optimized for that case (i.e. everything is a stream and is implemented as a stream).

### How to proceed from here ... 

@cachedout You are right, the `odbt` test should definitely be done first as it will not use the entire code-path that is currently showing errors.

As we are dealing with a spurios error, I'd think that the reason for this must be either a data race or memory corruption. The latter is rather unlikely in the python world (at least if there no actual segfault), so lets evaluate the first. Ruling out multi-threading as cause for this, there might be inter-process data races. The question is:

* are there multiple tests running on the same machine at the same time ?
  * GitPython uses `tempfile` to get directory names to use for testing. It will only generate a name, which then has to be created on disk. In theory, the second process attempting to use an already existing tempdir will fail, besides the fact that clashes should be extremely unlikely. Anyway ;).

However, I wouldn't believe a data race is causing this, but if you say there are multiple tests running on the same machine, we might have something to test at least.

Assuming the error has everything to do with `smmap`, you might try to use a version that doesn't support python 3 - [v0.8.2](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/smmap/tree/v0.8.2) should be the one, it is expected to work with the latest `gitdb` version.

What I am going for here is clearly to bisect smmap (and possibly) gitdb versions to find a version that doesn't show the issue. To do that properly, it might be worth knowing what the last working version for you was. Maybe the diffs would show a clearer image.

We are just starting with Python, so perhaps our viewpoint will change in the future. Our first program, done only a month ago, was to modify the example command based program to drive a single motor on our prototyping system. We're pleased to say it worked well. So well, in fact, that we decided to try porting last season's program, which is C++ and command based. However, we have not gotten a chance to drive with it yet, though we hope to soon. (We're only meeting once a week during the off-season.)

Our team actually tried Python for Rebound Rumble but at that time they couldn't even get the program to start, so they went with C++. However, Python is now looking like a first-class citizen of the FRC ecosystem and many of the team programmers would rather use it. That being said, we have several years of experience with the command based paradigm and porting to something like magicbot is daunting. Here are a few use cases we have that we don't see an easy way to implement with magicbot, followed by few things we think mitigate the arguments against command based.

If there are existing solutions to the issues we've been puzzled by, listing them in the proposed warning message HTML page would probably help other teams looking to port their programs to Python.

# Command-Based Tricks
In no particular order

## Controller Flexibility
One of the really great things about the command based paradigms is that it makes controller coupling easy and flexible. The syntax for assigning an action to a button is just `button.event(Command())`. By contrast, the example given for magicbot is five lines:

```
try:
    if self.joystick.getTrigger():
        self.component.doSomething()
except:
    self.onException()
```

We have gotten into the habit of creating multiple controller layouts for different situations. For example, during development we just assign every last button on a controller to something, because it's fast for testing. At competitions we generally have two controllers (with two drivers), and we try to use buttons that are far away from each other to avoid accidentally triggering the wrong action (a lesson learned the hard way, sadly). For open houses we only want visitors to be able to drive the robot (slowly) and do a few simple actions that can't cause any damage.

With C++ we could include different files for each occasion. With Python we plan to run different functions in `OI` depending on the situation. To do the same with magic bot it looks like we'd either need to have conditional logic inside of `teleopPeriodic()` or assign different functions to `teleopPeriodic`.

With magicbot's seemingly much more verbose syntax for acting on a button press, that means we'd have at least five times as many lines, and possibly some extra logic checks on every tick of `teleopPeriodic`. That would make it a bit harder to see, at a glance, what action is assigned to a button.

## Event Flexibility
The button framework, which ties in nicely with commands, has many events that can trigger (or cancel) a command. They are `whenPressed`, `whileHeld`, `whenReleased`, `toggleWhenPressed`, and `cancelWhenPressed`. These open up a lot of possibilities. For example, we have "modes" that are activated by pressing a button and deactivated by pressing it again using `toggleWhenPressed`. In the past we've used every button event except for `whenReleased`, though there are definitely some interesting use cases for it as well.

We don't understand exactly how button presses are related to actions in the magicbot examples, but it appears to either be the equivalent of `whenPressed` or `whileHeld`. The more complex options seem to be possible, but it also seems like they'd require more code and be less readable.

## Concurrency
For shooting at a high goal, we need to rotate our robot to face the goal, move the shooter to the correct angle, and spin up the firing wheels. We could do them one at at time, but a desire to squeeze out every last second of performance led us to run them all in parallel. Since all three actions use sensors, there is no way to know which one will finish first or last, but we need all three to be finished before we launch the ball.

With command based, we can make three commands, place them in a command group to run in parallel, and add `fire` sequentially after that command group. It seems like that would be possible to program with magicbot state machines, but it would be more verbose and becomes increasingly complex as more actions happen in parallel.

## Conflict Management
In a well written command based program, there is little danger of experimenting with commands. The scheduler ensures that commands that would conflict cannot be run in parallel. For example, if I am spinning the pickup system inward to pick up a ball and I try to run a command that spits it out, the scheduler will automatically stop the first command and give it a chance to clean up after itself before starting the second.

Being mindful of what your code is doing is important, but mistakes happen. Is there any mechanism in magicbot to make sure that different states or actions aren't using a motor that is being controlled by something else?

## Reordering Steps
Every year we find that our initial plan would work better if steps were moved around. Since our commands are inside a command group, scheduled with `addSequential` and `addParallel`, it is often only a matter of dragging a line from one place to another. Anyone looking at the group can easily read through it and see when each command is triggered, since they always run from top to bottom.

It appears that in magicbot this is not the case. The methods in a state machine are ordered using a decorator. It is possible to skip a method or have them run out of order. That offers a great amount of flexibility and opens up interesting possibilities, but it is not easily grasped. Punishing people for not reading a decorator correctly is certainly fun, but not something we want to have happen during a last minute scramble in the pits.

## Dashboard Feedback
Using the SmartDashboard, we can always see what commands our robot is currently running and cancel them if desired. We can also place buttons on the dashboard to run certain commands (generally commands that only get run in special circumstances instead of during a match).

Does magicbot provide any SmartDashboard feedback?

# Possible Mitigations of Command Based

## Too Much Boilerplate
Coming from C++ we have built up an abnormally high tolerance for boilerplate, but even there we, being the lazy people we are, were always trying to find ways to type less. Since Python is much more flexible, it would seem that it would have even better options to eliminate boilerplate.

It appears that someone has already started on this, since the Command class has its abstract methods `initialize`, `execute`, `end`, and `interrupted` replaced with empty methods, meaning that someone implementing a Command that doesn't need one of those can simply ignore it. In our code base we went a step further and created base classes for different types of commands we frequently use. That way we only need to implement the special cases.

When subclassing `IterativeRobot`, the periodic functions are the same for all modes (except Test) in command based, so we use the same method for all of them, resulting in less duplicated code. Someone feeling especially clever could even do something like `self.teleopPeriodic = wpilib.command.Scheduler.getInstance().run` and not implement those methods at all, though there are good reasons not to go that route.

Subsystems don't have much boilerplate to them, so with just a few simple changes we feel like we've dramatically reduced the amount of boilerplate we're typing. If other teams are interested, we'd be happy to try to generalize what we've done (once it's a bit more battle-tested) and it could be added to robotpy-wpilib-utilities.

## An Exception Will Kill Your Program
Exceptions are going to happen, even in strongly typed languages like Java and C++. The roboRIO knows this and will restart the program if it detects a crash, so in some sense the current situation isn't too bad.

But, it could be better. The great thing about command based is that almost all interesting actions happen inside `Scheduler.run()`. If we wrap that method in a try/except block, we can largely guarantee that a last minute change won't kill the robot outright. Additionally, because the scheduler has access to all running commands we can easily cancel the commands that were running and might now be in an inconsistent state.

# Conclusion
Command Based, magicbot, and even SimpleRobot all have their places. They provide different ways of approaching problems and make some things better and other things worse. Adding a new one is great, but deprecating an existing and recommended one without a clear migration path seems ill advised.

Obviously, we are very, very new to programming the robot with Python. However, over the past month we've tried to learn a lot and consider the different options. Hopefully our questions will spur improved documentation and make robotpy-wpilib an even better starting place for FRC teams.

Since we're only meeting once a week currently, we may not have much to add until next Wednesday when we, hopefully, get to test our ported driving code on our actual robot.

Scalars are stored by deepdish as attributes in HDF5. This includes Python regular scalars (e.g. `100`), Numpy scalars (e.g. `np.int32(100)`), and Numpy array scalars (e.g. `np.array(100)`). They will all be read as a Numpy scalar. So, as far as I can tell, deepdish never stores arrays of shape `()`, so I see no problem in adding this behavior to unpack them to deepdish. Reading non-deepdish HDF5 well is a goal of deepdish.

I just noticed one thing I haven't considered until now. If you store a very large integer (>64 bits), then it will be stored as a `np.array(1234234..., dtype=object)`. This is actually a numpy array of shape `()`, so I take back what I said. Fixing this would also fix the big integer problem. Thanks for pointing this out and offering a solution. I will push a commit with your suggested fix (unless you want to submit it as a PR).

It seems like the next step would be to feed into something like a least squares solver, which also works in observations x predictors space.

The use case would be for fitting, e.g., a collection of linear models. For example, you have a number of raw features (e.g., physical variables) defined on a grid (latitude, longitude, time). You combine these raw features with patsy to build predictors, also on the same grid. Then you a bunch of independent time series models on (time, feature) for each (latitude, longitude) grid point.

This sort of analysis is pretty common in the geosciences, e.g., to understand climate trends.

It occurs to me that we can't assume that the normalization of `ChromaticObject`s with `.SED = None` is unity.  For example, the following is a contrived, but presumably valid, chromatic PSF:

```
f1 = lambda w: w
f2 = lambda w: 1-w

psf1 = galsim.Gaussian(fwhm=1) * f1  # Makes a ChromaticTransformation
psf2 = galsim.Gaussian(fwhm=2) * f2

psf = psf1 + psf2
```

The final psf above is properly normalized, but the components are not.  The components still need to be constructible though.  So I think this implies that `ChromaticObject`s need both a `.SED` attribute and a `._flux_ratio` attribute, though presumably only one or the other should be meaningful (or even non-`None`) for a given object.

It also occurs to me that instead of keeping track of both a `._flux_ratio` and a `.SED`, we could instead have both `ChromaticObject`s and `FluxedChromaticObject`s (something like this might work for distinguishing `GSObject`s too).  That would be kind of a big change, but I think it's one that would be mostly transparent to users, since they presumably interact mostly with the derived classes and not `ChromaticObject` or `GSObject` directly.  I'm not currently planning on implementing this here, but I'm curious what others' thoughts are.

Here are things that I think probably ought to be done before this is ready to submit a PR.
1. Add the poisson solver code the can make vertex files, so users can run this themselves if desired.
2. Give some sensible instructions to the user if the vertex file is not found.  e.g. what functions to run to build a vertex file.  Rather than the current IOError.
3. Consider making a few more things python-settable parameters.  It looks like there are still a few hard-coded numbers in the code.  Like I think the sensor height is hard-coded to be 100 atm.  That should probably be made a construction-time parameter.  Ideally, any special number in the code that isn't just math ought to be pulled out to the python layer so the user can change it.
4. Settle on a good set of parameters for LSST, which we can name LSST_silicon.cfg or something like that.  I don't know which of the config files currently in devel/poisson is the one to use.  The tests use numvertices_4.  But I think you said you wanted to tweak some parameters to get the slopes a bit closer to matching lab data.  So once you have something you're reasonably happy with, you should give it a name indicating that it is the right thing for people to use for LSST sims.
5. Move that final file and its vertices file to the GalSim/share directory so they get installed to the share directory when people install GalSim.  Also, make a variable in `galsim/lsst/__init__.py` with the file name.  We don't have anything there yet, but look at `galsim/wfirst/__init__.py` to see what I mean.  Something like `silicon_config_file = 'LSST_silicon.cfg'` or whatever name you want to use.

Some other improvements that I think we can push off to a later issue.  Although fine if you feel inspired to tackle any of them now.
1. I think I'd like to encapsulate the information in the Silicon config file into a class rather than just have it be read in from a file.  That would make it easier for the user to access the values and maybe make a new configuration that is based on the existing one, but with a few changed values.
2. The I/O of the vertex file could be sped up significantly by writing and reading binary fits tables rather than ASCII.  I doubt the speed here is a big concern though, so this probably isn't that important.
3. It would be nice if the user didn't have to specify the location of the vertex file.  e.g. if one of the attributes of the configuration object was the name of the file.
4. I'd like to take a stab at doing some optimization of the code.  I have a few ideas about how some of the functions could be sped up.  But I'll wait to do a proper profiling of it to see where the hot spots are and so where optimizations would help the most.
5. Make all of this runnable from a GalSim config file.  This definitely ought to be its own issue, which I'd be happy to tackle.
6. Make a new demo for LSST simulations that showcase how to use this new functionality.  That would probably be appropriate to do along side (5).

Anything I'm missing?

As I am currently facing testing of a connexion api myself, here is a quick overview of my "setup" so far.
Feedback greatly appreciated, would love to add to the docs.

## 1. Unit Tests
Using e.g. [Tox](http://tox.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) or similar to provide a virtual env, test your units (functions) by [mocking](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pytest-mock) everything needed.

## 2. Integration Tests
Test the respective function calls/routes individually. Mock database etc.

## 3. End to End Tests
Test everything in conjunction. Populate your API models with fake/dummy data and cross-test every resource.

The Unit Test part most definitely needs to be done completely by hand, but for step 2 [Dredd](http://dredd.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html) by @apiaryio looks very promising.

### Dredd
If I get it right, you can simply add some example values to your swagger/openAPI specification and some hooks and Dredd will automatically validate your routes.

E2E-testing probably needs to be done by hand again.

---

What do you guys do? What do you think of my testing approach? Where can it be improved?
I really don't like the 'need to write everything by hand' approach as swagger provides such a nice overview of all routes, that should be leveraged.

Thanks for using OpenFisca and reaching to us.

As of today, `openfisca-serve` is more suited for local tests than production. It uses a builtin python server which performances are not great.

We recommend to use `gunicorn`, with a proper configuration file such as [this one](https://github.com/sgmap/mes-aides-ui/blob/master/openfisca/api_config.ini).

You can then run your server with the command:

```sh
gunicorn  --paster openfisca/api_config.ini
```

However, if you're interested in running calculations, we are currently working on a new calculation web API and would love to have your feedback on it :).

I am happy to see the interest in my Pull Request and the discussion here (https://github.com/burke-software/django-mass-edit/pull/64). I would like to address the points raised:

1. Although opening multiple tabs in not a common scenario in submit based admin actions and the integrity of the session data important only for the time of the redirect I updated my Pull request to use a hash of the object ids string as per @PetrDlouhy 's suggestion. (https://github.com/burke-software/django-mass-edit/pull/62/commits/9c90eda9c4b84533cf6c4b393e0a1292b7faaa6f)

2. I would like to argue in favor of my approach to use the session storage only if the URL is longer than a certain length: In small data sets I can see the use of bookmarking or sharing the URL to mass edit a collection of items. This becomes irrelevant in larger growing tables. Furthermore, it keeps backward compatibility for anyone who have already bookmarked such a set.

3. Sessions are enabled by default when using django-admin and I am almost sure they are required for use with django auth.

The final step was commenting out the original code in the (DEBUG==0) branch, and adding the same code as in the (DEBUG==1) branch. @matsjoyce: if you were referring to the working situation with pickle that I alluded to, I just replaced dill with pickle in the above example.

when you `>>> import dill`, then everything `dill` knows how to pickle is added to the `pickle` registry.  So, yes, that's the expected behavior.  `dill` tells `pickle` how to handle most objects, and you don't have to do anything else.  To toggle this behavior explicitly, you can use `dill.extend(True)` or `False`.

So if the original text has "мёдом", and the human-typed search string is "мед" (which as you know is a pretty common case for non-academic texts to substitute one for another), the two stemmed words will not match even if the stemmer properly stems the former to "мёд".

Just to repeat I'm not talking here about solving my immediate problem which I have already solved by doing the normalization myself, but rather about making the library more friendly to the new users and easier to use. First, you need to introduce an appropriate converter class into the JPMML-SkLearn project:
1. Create a subclass of `sklearn.Transformer`, and implement the conversion "business logic" in the `Transformer#encodeFeatures(List, List, FeatureMapper)` method.
2. Register this class with `org.jpmml.sklearn.PickleUtil`.

There's not much documentation about it. Here's an example about implementing a converter class for Scikit-Learn's `FunctionTransformer` transformation type:
https://github.com/jpmml/jpmml-sklearn/commit/5c4a181cd70d06c8179586cb3be0c881a9ba02e0

Then, build your modified JPMML-SkLearn project with Apache Maven, and drop the resulting JAR file into the sklearn2pmml `/resources/` directory. Currently, you would be replacing `jpmml-sklearn-1.1.4.jar` with your own `jpmml-sklearn-1.1-SNAPSHOT.jar`.

If you want you can also install the package as is it currently on the master branch, with the following command:
pip install git+git://github.com/googlemaps/google-maps-services-python@master
This should give you the channel parameter.

Side note: as it is currently on master, you can only use the channel arg if you don't use the key arg at all. I submitted a new pull request to correct that (i.e. allow channel when client ID and key are both used).

I am not really familiar with how you are serving static files. I think the routing logic in the `replay.py` has grown messy and monolithic over the time.

The Reconstrcutive module provides logs to track down any issues, while you can also log requests in in the `replay.py` to see where those requests are hitting.

> If I recall, the only reason we request webui.js is to allow the serviceWorker to register from a URI-M if the main ipwb page is not initially hit to register the SW.

Any request that is routed using the Reconstrcutive module, and not excluded, has a request header (like `X-ServiceWorker: Reconstrcutive:0.1`) in it. If this is missing (or undesired version) in a request that is not a local resource (i.e., it is supposed to be handled by the SW), then the server can redirect user where the appropriate SW will be installed.

> There was also a conversation about adding the ability to inject an archive banner for navigation using this injected script.

There is a placeholder function (which can be overwritten too) in the Reconstrcutive module to specify custom banner. The ability is there, but a default banner is not implemented yet.

XHR is deprecated with SWs, only `fetch()` can be used for AJAX, which limits the response headers we can retrieve. Because we are storing the ipwb version in a response header that fetch() cannot retrieve, attempting to do so might not be a viable way of getting the current version of the SW from within the SW JS.

This looks like a solid approach but I'll point the following out.  The `StateDB` object is ephemeral in that it comes into existence as a context manager when accessing the state is necessary and then is discarded after.  That means that the `ChainDB` will need to be responsible for persisting the touched keys.  This *may* be fine if the `db` instance passed into the `StateDB` is where the tracking occurs, at which point the `StateDB` can be blissfully unaware that all of the keys it touches are being tracked.

> I can not wrap my head around this mysterious perl 18.2, \[...\] Could this be a  reason for the problem?

Yes, that's indeed the problem. The `conda list` output (thanks for providing it!) shows that at one point you must've installed a bunch of Perl packages from the `dan_blanchard` channel, including `perl 5.18.2=8` which the others depend on.

Your `conda config` output shows a few setting that I'd advice you to change:
* `ssl_verify: False` shouldn't usually be set all the time, rather just for troubleshooting.
  You can remove it by running
  `conda config --remove-key ssl_verify`
* You have `r` in the channel list, but the packages from there are included in `defaults` with `https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/r` (as you can see from your `conda info` output).
  You can remove it with
  `conda config --remove channels r`.
* You have `https://conda.binstar.org/faircloth-lab` in your channel list. Looks like that channel mostly includes (older versions of) packages also available in `bioconda`/`conda-forge`.
  If you don't need it, you can remove it with
  `conda config --remove channels 'https://conda.binstar.org/faircloth-lab'`
* The channel priorities of your configuration are `[bioconda, defaults, conda-forge]`. The official recommendation for Bioconda is `[bioconda, conda-forge, defaults]`.
  You can change that by running
  `conda config --add channels conda-forge --add channels bioconda`

More information regarding that last point:
About a year ago the [Bioconda documentation on setting up channel order](http://bioconda.github.io/#set-up-channels) were updated to recommend giving `conda-forge` a higher priority than `defaults`. This is because `bioconda` builds on top of `conda-forge`'s packages now.
But note that it is generally not advised, whenever possible, to mix and match packages from `conda-forge` and `defaults`, due to binary incompatibilities (though, the situation for this will likely improve in near future). This also applies (partially due to other reasons) to packages installed by `pip` (seeing that you also have some packages marked as installed by `<pip>` in your list) -- if the packages you need are available as Conda packages, you would want to prefer those over `pip install`ing those form PyPI.

After you updated your channel configuration with those `conda config` commands, you can try to run `conda update --all` which **might** cause those `dan_blanchard` packages to be replaced by `conda-forge` ones. If that doesn't help (which is not unlikely), one of the easiest solutions is just to start over with a fresh new Miniconda3 (or Anaconda3) installation. Regardless whether you start over again or not, I strongly advice you to read up on https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/tasks/manage-environments.html . Essentially, this allows you to create separate environment for different tools or workflows with which you can avoid version conflicts or those binary incompatibilities between `defaults` and `conda-forge`. You can even have per-environment configurations by using `conda config --env your-env-name`.

#!/usr/bin/env python
import subprocess

##### pySAM cannot use new AlignmentFile headers when paired read information is
##### present in a multi reference input file. This script splits an input SAM

##### those reads are mapped, but . . .

pair_status = ['=',"1","60"]
# New single reference header for a single reference AlignmentFile
use_single_header = True

### . . . succeeds:

## Original multi-reference header for a single reference AlignmentFile
#use_single_header = False

### . . . succeeds:
## without paired read mapped info
#pair_status = ['*',"0","0"]
## New single reference header for a single reference AlignmentFile
#use_single_header = False

# make a simple SAM file to test with

f.write('\t'.join(["read1","2","chrA","1","60","10M"] + pair_status + \
        ["GAGCAAAAGC","AB@?ABFFB4\n"]))
f.write('\t'.join(["read2","2","chrB","1","60","10M"] + pair_status + \
        ["GAGCAAAAGC","AB@?ABFFB4"]))
f.close()

# BAM and index it so we can fetch reads per chromosome (reference sequence)

pysam.index("foo.bam")

bam_in = pysam.AlignmentFile("foo.bam")
print('Header dict of 2 reference seq sam file')
print(bam_in.header)

## make new SAM files with just one or other reference and its reads
# optionally make a header with just the included reference (determined
# by use_single_header)
for n,C in enumerate('AB'):
    if use_single_header:
        new_header = {}
        for k,v in bam_in.header.items():
            if k == 'SQ':
                new_header[k] = [v[n]]
            else:
                new_header[k] = v
        use_header = new_header
    else:
        use_header = bam_in.header
    
    print('Header for chr'+C, use_header)

    with pysam.AlignmentFile("bar_chr{}.sam".format(C), "w", header = use_header,
            add_sam_header = True) as sam_out:
        for read in bam_in.fetch('chr'+C):
            print('reference_id of read on chr{0} in bar_chr{0}.sam'.format(C),

            read.reference_id = 0
            sam_out.write(read)
        print('Finished writing to {}, about to close . . .'.format(

    pysam.view("-b", "-h", "-o", "bar_chr{0}.bam".format(C), "bar_chr{0}.sam".format(C), 
            catch_stdout = False)
    pysam.index("bar_chr{}.bam".format(C))

print('All done.')

I just solved the issue, there is a possible bug in the source.

I tried to pass the parameters like the document suggested, it works.

```
parameters=GenericResource(
        location='West US',
        properties={},
    )
```

But using parameters=resource does not work. Looking at the error from response.__dict__ in the azure\mgmt\resource\resources\operations\resources_operations.py create_or_update method, it throws "Message":"The parameter properties has an invalid value."

From the resource, the properties was None, which I assume should be fine. But it expects {}.

I fixed the code by adding 
```
if not resource.properties:
    resource.properties = {}
```

Hi @eligne-pro, each time you create a service (i.e. BlockBlobService), we keep using the same [requests.Session](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/api/#request-sessions) object to make the requests, since Session takes care of connection pooling and also allows the reusing of the underlying TCP connections to the same host. This is great for [performance](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#session-objects), and makes sense in most scenarios, but unfortunately except in your case. 

Since we already use the `requests` module, and our library is open-source, it would actually be really easy to make the change that you are suggesting. Basically, you need to change [this part](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/blob/master/azure/storage/_http/httpclient.py#L108) to not use the Session object. So you could build your own version of this library for the specific scenario that you are encountering.

I will raise your suggestion with my colleagues, but cannot promise anything.

Please let me know if you have any question. 😃

The problem with `requirements.txt` is it could be infinitely expanded (what if that `requirements.txt` itself contains `-r`?).  How would pre-commit know when to invalidate an environment?

`-r requirements.txt` is also purely a convention.  You could have `-r foo.bar` just as well -- I don't think it makes sense to special case "files ending in .txt".

In general, `requirements.txt` is not supposed to be used for libraries anyway (there shouldn't be duplication in `setup.py` in that case).

Do you have a specific usecase in mind?  I'd like to see something concrete before even trying to think of how this would be implemented :)

Just want to say that I really like this idea of a writeable collection of `all_departed_units` and `broken_relations`. I think this is really useful!

That said, I'm not convinced that it's good to keep `relation.departed_units`. We risk Charmers writing code like the following:

```python
for rel in self.relations:
    for un in rel.departed_units:
        # handle departed unit
        # remove departed unit from collection
```

Which will work for every departed unit except the last one. The only correct way to use `departed_units` is the following:

```python
for rel in self.relations:
    for un in rel.departed_units:
        # handle departed unit
        # remove departed unit from collection
for rel in self.broken_relations:
    for un in rel.departed_units:
        # handle departed unit
        # remove departed unit from collection
```

Which is a lot less cleaner than

```python
for un in self.all_departed_units:
    # handle departed unit
    # remove departed unit from collection
```

Moreover, if a charmer really wants to iterate departed units of a single relation, he can always do so by filtering `self.departed_units`. This is a little bit more complex, but I don't think it's a common use-case so I think that's a good tradeoff for preventing a charmer from shooting itself in the foot.

**Smal note**

Just an idea that popped into my head: what do you think about making an alias `self.joined_relations` for `self.relations` and pointing to `joined_relations` in the docs? We could do the same with `relation.joined_units`. This makes it explicit, useful for charmers who don't read the docs.

The flag should probably be driven by `len(self.all_units) > 0` rather than `len(self.relations) > 0` but even so it won't be cleared during the `-departed` hook since the unit is still there, as @ajkavanagh said.

The fundamental problem is that Satellite's APIs (and the APIs of its constituent parts, like Katello) are extremely inconsistent. Nailgun tries to hide these inconsistencies by constructing search requests in one particular way by default and then making exceptions as necessary. Unfortunately, the work-arounds aren't comprehensive, and the underlying issues bubble up.

`Entity().search_payload(...)` shows the search query that's constructed.

test1.py
```python
import socket

sender = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)

```

test2.py
```python
import socket
sender = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
sender.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
sender.bind(('51.254.204.93', 8000))
```

Hi @jo-tham 

I'm not part of the marshmallow team but, since I was mentioned, I can help you out in an unofficial capacity.

I don't believe nesting schemas are the correct answer.  My opinion is that the schema should be a flat structure.  It should be exactly as your model is (with obvious derivations).  You can use `pre_load` and `post_dump` decorators to structure and flatten the requests and responses as you see fit.

I ended up not using this library.  I wrote my own implementation.  It requires two very simple components:

`BaseSchema` schema:
* `pre_load` method to flatten the JSON API 1.0 request object
* `post_dump` method to structure the flat data structure into the JSON API 1.0 request object
* Hijack the _do_load hook so you can format errors according to the JSON API 1.0 specification 

`Relationship` field:
* Inherits from fields.Field
* Overload the _serializer and _deserialize methods

You're done!  Don't bother with the `type_` in the class meta.  Just create a required field with a default and a trivial amount of validation.

Yes there is an issue with the converter currently. It does not handle "TimeDistributed()" functions properly. 

In CoreML, there is always a sequence dimension (see [documentation](https://apple.github.io/coremltools/coremlspecification/sections/NeuralNetwork.html))
So unlike Keras, there is no need to specify "TimeDistributed()" separately. 
The converter thus sort of ignores the "TimeDistributed()" keyword, but here the 4-D shape to a convolution (when it expects 3-D) is throwing the converter off. 

A workaround would be to build a different Keras model for the purpose of conversion (using `get_weights()` and `set_weights()` functions you should be able to transfer the weights from the actual trained Keras model to this new one for conversion)

```
model = Sequential()
model.add(Convolution2D(16, 3, 3, border_mode="same", activation='relu', input_shape=(100, 100, 3)))
model.add(MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2, 2)))
model.add(Convolution2D(32, 3, 3, activation='relu'))
model.add(MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2, 2)))
model.add(Convolution2D(64, 3, 3, activation='relu'))
model.add(MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2, 2)))
model.add(Convolution2D(128, 3, 3, activation='relu'))
model.add(MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2, 2)))
model.add(Flatten())
model.add(Reshape((1,-1))) #seq length = 1
model.add(LSTM(256, return_sequences=True))
model.add(Dense(1))
```

Optionals are meant to have spaces around them. They're for "optional words", not "or".

The regular expression the optionals turn into makes use of the `\b` word-boundary metacharacter, which matches on characters that aren't "word characters" (numbers or letters), so if the optional is directly touching a word, no word boundary is detected and it doesn't match properly.

You could use an array for common suffixes. With `! array s = s es`, the trigger syntax `@s` would get expanded into the non-capturing regular expression `(?:s|es)`, so it should work fine directly touching other words in your trigger, like

```
+ fox@s

# the regexp would become:

Thanks for the feedback @kingosticks. My response is a bit long so I'll break it into sections:

**Current features:**

1. I think this PR could be the first step towards getting rid of the **Streams page** (as discussed in #75). The only feature that that page provides that is not available elsewhere at this stage is the 'Get currently playing' button. This is maybe also something that we would want to add to the context menu so that users can get the URI for any track whenever it is displayed on screen (in other words a 'Copy' entry like you would see in most other application context menus). If we agree on that then we can create an issue to track moving that feature.

2. **Playlists support** has probably been left behind a little recently, but that is what #164 is for. I've been putting it off in favor of working on the smaller 'power user' features specifically because code re-use is so difficult and it seems every new feature becomes a bit of a copy and paste exercise. The codebase needs to be refactored to make reuse easier and the only way I would feel comfortable with a wholesale refactor myself is if we have proper test coverage for everything. We're not there yet and a lot of the javascript still needs to be modularized to make testing possible.

**Direction going forward:**

As far as the direction of the MMW project is concerned: I think there are a bunch of really good looking and easy to use frontends out there, with new ones starting up all the time.  There are simple frontends for older devices, custom-built ones for touch screens, frontends that focus on mobile, frontends that aim to enhance the overall music experience by pulling in additional content, frontends built to integrate closely with Spotify, and really attractive frontends built around the aesthetics of the UI. 

I like MMW because it works just as well on mobile as it does on the desktop, and most of the Mopidy features that I care about are only a swipe or a click away. My feeling is that the niche that we should be aiming for is to offer a flexible, full featured, generic web frontend that allows experienced users to get the most out of their Mopidy server. I suspect that a lot of people were introduced to Mopidy as a result of the MusicBox project (I know I was), and if there will be more releases of MusicBox in the future then the features for editing settings and managing the system will be really important too in order to lower the barrier of entry. 

**Unified interface:**

We could probably get away with no more than four buttons on-screen in the top right corner at any given time (these split into groups of two on small screens), and use the context menus for the balance. This works well for giving the user access to the most commonly used features all of the time while keeping the UI relatively uncluttered by tucking the other options away in an off-screen menu. There will be a limit to the number of entries that the context menu can contain before the array of options become bewildering but I don't think we've reached that point yet.

@woutervanwijk did great work coming up with the UI in the first place, which is what attracted me to MMW initially. Perhaps he can chime in with a few thoughts as well...

Correct, there is no API function for quadruple precision floats, but it is not necessary. Like I said before, the "usual" way is to use three bit-vectors to construct numerals via the `(fp ...)` constructor in SMT2 and the corresponding C API function is `Z3_mk_fpa_fp`. There was a spurious assertion failure for these large numbers, but I just fixed that one. 

For example, in SMT2 language, this here:

    (set-logic QF_FP)
    (set-option :pp.fp_real_literals true)
    (declare-fun x () (_ FloatingPoint 15 113))
    (assert (= x (fp #b0 #b011111111111111 #x8000000000000000000000000000)))
    (check-sat)
    (get-model)

produces

    (model 
      (define-fun x () (_ FloatingPoint 15 113)
        ((_ to_fp 15 113) RTZ 1.5 0))
    )

The construction of numerals via bit-vectors is always fast because there is no actual conversion to be computed, as internally Z3 uses a 3 bit-vector representation as well.

However, the bit-blasting that happens later (conversion to SAT) can be inefficient or hard to solve too, especially when complicated operations are used on large floats, i.e., keep them as small as possible (prefer single over double if the precision isn't required). As a rough example of what to expect: symbolic representation of the float remainder `a % b` where `a`, `b` are double-precision, produces a SAT formula that is larger than 2 GB. The remainder is by far the worst of all operations though.

In general it's important to remembre that floating-point numbers are _not_ a cheap version of reals. Quite often the floating-point equivalent of a real numbered problem is much harder to solve, and also vice versa, sometimes the real-numbered equivalent of a floating-point problem can be harder to solve too. But, the very worst case is when floats are converted to reals and vice versa within one problem.

The way that feature works is that, it'll ask you to confirm to see the results if the number of rows in the result set is above the threshold you've set in the env var.

For example: If you have `MSSQL_CLI_ROW_LIMIT` set to 10 and you run a query that returns 11 rows then the client will prompt you with a question that asks `The result has 11 rows are you sure you want to see the results (y/N)?`. 

The default value for the row limit is 1000 which is set in the config file located under `C:\Users\<Username>\AppData\Local\dbcli\mssqlcli\config`. Just look for row_limit and you can change it there as well.

Hi @MichaelHanssonPH ,
When looking into the polarized snapshot attached, it could actually be an issue of endianness as it looks like that the upper and the lower bytes data are swapped.
The depth raw data that is stored arrives in LSB order, while according to this [spec](https://www.w3.org/TR/REC-png-961001) PNG expects the 16bit pixel to be MSB-aligned
> ...16-bit samples are stored in network byte order (MSB first).

We'll look further into this as it seem to be a software issue. Meanwhile you can try to reverse the endianness or just treat the raw data as a binary stream rather than PNG image.

I investigated a little of this issue. The problem seems to exist only on Django 1.9 and later, most probably because of the following code (reference to [utils.py](https://github.com/jazzband/django-debug-toolbar/blob/master/debug_toolbar/utils.py#L117-L142)):

````python
def get_template_context(node, context, context_lines=3):
    source = getattr(node, 'source', None)
    # In Django 1.9 template Node does not have source property, Origin does
    # not reload method, so we extract contextual information from exception
    # info.
    if source:
        line, source_lines, name = get_template_source_from_source(source)
    else:
        line, source_lines, name = get_template_source_from_exception_info(
            node, context)

The dual-argument `init` is for deciding between the constructor paths to follow based on how the constructing class is being used in Python, but *not* based on the arguments to that constructor (which is why identical argument types are enforced).  So the first one gets invoked when the Python caller is initializing the class directly, while the second one gets invoked when the class is being initialized as a based class of a Python subclass initialization.

If you want overload resolution--i.e. to do something differently when the *argument* is a different type, then you need to use an overload, as you provided.  Though I think you'll have to move the trampoline constructor first, however, so that it gets tried before the based class constructor.

There are actually *four* possible cases to consider (and so you might want to use two overloads, each providing a pair of init functions):
- class invoked directly, argument is a trampoline instance
- class invoked via Python inheritance, argument is a trampoline instance
- class invoked directly, argument is not a trampoline
- class invoked via Python inheritance, argument is not a trampoline
Your overloads would first decide between (1,2) or (3,4), then the dual-init would let your code distinguish between (1,2) on the first overload and (3, 4) on the second.

It's quite possible you don't need to have 4 separate lambdas, however: case 1 and 3, for instance, are probably going to be identical (i.e. invoking the base copy constructor, whether or not the argument is actually a trampoline instance).

Or, alternatively, you might want invocation from a trampolined instance to produce a trampoline even when a trampoline isn't needed, in which case 2 and 3 will be the same.
