Yes - the issue is that you manually deleted the cert file (probably with a command like `rm`)
Unfortunately a reference to it is still in your nginx config file.
Before we run certbot we check that your current webserver configuration is valid.
as seen in this line: `nginx: configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf test failed`
The issue is that when we check the validity of the conf file we check to make sure all files referenced in it exist, in this case your old certificate no longer exists on your hard drive, as seen here:
`(SSL: error:02001002:system library:fopen:No such file or directory:fopen('/etc/letsencrypt/live/xyz.xyz.pl/fullchain.pem','r') error:2006D080:BIO routines:BIO_new_file:no such file)`
So to get certbot to run with nginx again, you'll have to remove all references to your old certificate from your nginx config file.
Let me know if that works!

> Why is this just failing now?

Travis probably changed something upstream. If I remember correctly, we're using an undocumented feature of Travis where different tests run on different environments. The environments that need `sudo` are slower, but we need it in some cases and wanted to avoid using the slower environments for all builds.

> How are our tests passing without it?

Because we specify `sudo` for each job that needs it ([source](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/.travis.yml#L48)). It's my guess we never needed `sudo` here. I can share another use case. I'm evaluating Ray for a vision/robotics application. In my use case, I want to efficiently transfer fresh sets of camera images, point clouds, etc... between stages in a data processing pipeline including 1-2 learned stages running on GPU, and 3-4 stages (written in C++) running on the CPU. There's a 300 ms soft real-time deadline for this pipeline. I would like to log all of the raw data and many intermediate results to disk in parallel without disturbing the real-time data processing pipeline. For my image set, compressing and dumping on the main thread takes about 200ms. Pickling and transferring to another process takes about 100ms. Using Plasma and Arrow I hope I can get the data off the main thread in a trivial amount of time (10 ms?) and into another non-realtime (in the linux scheduler sense) thread or process. If Ray supported prioritization (or an even more direct representation of the deadlines as @atumanov suggests), I could consider using it for setting up the whole pipeline, including data recording.

A couple related things.

One is the PR #249 which enabled pushing error messages to specific drivers from C. It was closed, but something like that would be useful.

Second, we try to catch most exceptions that could happen on a worker process with by using `try` and `except` within the worker. If we missed something, then there is also a `try` `except` around the entire worker main loop, which will catch errors that are otherwise unhandled and will push an error to the relevant driver. https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/workers/default_worker.py#L51

One thing to keep in mind is that when a worker dies, the task it was supposed to be executing presumably did not finish, so the task return values were not created. This means that other processes (a driver or other workers) may be blocked waiting for the task to finish. We can retry the task, but assuming it keeps killing workers, we should at some point give up and raise a python exception in the blocked processes.

Resolved by #133.

Actually, since the information *is* included in the metadata inside the product archive files in `MTD_MSIL2A.xml` and the Data Hub's OData API allows such individual files to be fetched from the archive files, you actually can retrieve this field and filter based on it, if you are really keen on it.

Here's a sample script that does just that:
```python
from sentinelsat import SentinelAPI
from xml.etree import ElementTree as ET

api = SentinelAPI('user', 'password')

# Query a single arbitrary product just so we have something to test with
products = api.query(raw='S2A_MSIL2A_20180614T084601_N0208_R107_T35SPB_20180614T120207')

def get_odata_file_url(uuid, path):
    odata_path = api.api_url + "odata/v1/Products('{}')".format(uuid)
    for p in path.split('/'):
        odata_path += "/Nodes('{}')".format(p)
    odata_path += '/$value'
    return odata_path

def fetch_s2_qi_info(uuid, product_name):
    path = '{}.SAFE/MTD_MSIL2A.xml'.format(product_name)
    url = get_odata_file_url(uuid, path)
    response = api.session.get(url)
    xml = ET.XML(response.content)
    qi_info = {}
    for elem in xml.find('.//Image_Content_QI'):
        qi_info[elem.tag] = float(elem.text)
    return qi_info

filtered = []
for uuid, metadata in products.items():
    qi_info = fetch_s2_qi_info(uuid, metadata['title'])
    if qi_info['NODATA_PIXEL_PERCENTAGE'] < 10:
        filtered.append(uuid)
```

This use case is probably a bit too specific to be included in sentinelsat, though, but we could consider adding a convenience function for the fetching of single files from product archives perhaps.
